seoTitle: Rubycon Technical Support | Application Notes | Authorized Distributor
seoDescription: Technical support for Rubycon capacitors including selection guides, application notes, FAQs, and engineering support. Contact our FAE team for assistance with distributor selection.

faqs.question: How do I select the right Rubycon capacitor for my application?
faqs.answer: Selecting the right Rubycon capacitor involves evaluating several key parameters. First, determine the required capacitance value based on your circuit requirements. Next, select a voltage rating with appropriate derating - typically 20-30% above your maximum operating voltage. Consider the operating temperature range and select a series rated for your application's temperature extremes. For switching power supplies, calculate the ripple current requirements and select a capacitor with adequate ripple current rating and thermal characteristics. Lifetime requirements are critical - Rubycon offers standard, long-life, and ultra-long-life series. Physical constraints such as size, mounting type (radial, snap-in, screw terminal), and lead spacing must also be considered. Our FAE team can assist with detailed calculations and selection guidance.
faqs.decisionGuide: Contact our FAE team with your application specifications for personalized component recommendations.

faqs.question: What technical documentation is available for Rubycon capacitors?
faqs.answer: Rubycon provides comprehensive technical documentation including datasheets, application notes, reliability data, and qualification reports. Datasheets contain detailed electrical specifications, mechanical dimensions, and performance characteristics. Application notes cover topics such as capacitor selection, ripple current calculations, thermal management, and soldering guidelines. Reliability data includes lifetime prediction models, failure rate information, and accelerated life test results. For automotive applications, PPAP documentation and AEC-Q200 qualification reports are available. LiTong provides access to all Rubycon technical documentation and can assist with interpretation and application-specific questions.
faqs.decisionGuide: Contact our sales team to request specific technical documentation for your project.

faqs.question: Does LiTong provide FAE support for Rubycon products?
faqs.answer: Yes, LiTong provides comprehensive FAE (Field Application Engineering) support for Rubycon products. Our FAE team has extensive experience with Rubycon capacitors across various applications including power supplies, motor drives, automotive, and consumer electronics. Support services include component selection assistance, thermal analysis, lifetime calculations, schematic review, and troubleshooting. We can provide sample support for evaluation, help interpret datasheets and specifications, and offer design recommendations. For complex applications, our FAEs can perform detailed analysis including ripple current calculations, thermal modeling, and reliability predictions. Contact our FAE team early in your design cycle for optimal results.
faqs.decisionGuide: Contact our FAE team to discuss your project and available support services.

faqs.question: How can I calculate capacitor lifetime for my application?
faqs.answer: Capacitor lifetime calculation follows the Arrhenius equation, which states that lifetime approximately doubles for every 10C decrease in temperature. The basic formula is: Lx = Lr x 2^((Tr-Tx)/10), where Lx is expected lifetime, Lr is rated lifetime at reference temperature Tr, and Tx is actual operating temperature. Voltage derating also affects lifetime - operating at 80% of rated voltage typically doubles lifetime compared to 100% voltage. Ripple current causes self-heating, which must be added to ambient temperature. Our FAE team can perform detailed lifetime calculations considering all these factors. Rubycon also provides online lifetime calculation tools for quick estimates.
faqs.decisionGuide: Contact our FAE team for detailed lifetime calculations specific to your operating conditions.

faqs.question: What is the lead time for Rubycon capacitors?
faqs.answer: Lead times for Rubycon capacitors vary based on product series, capacitance/voltage ratings, and market demand. Standard radial lead capacitors in popular values typically have lead times of 8-12 weeks. Snap-in capacitors generally range from 10-16 weeks depending on specifications. Specialized series such as automotive-grade or high-temperature capacitors may have longer lead times due to additional testing and qualification requirements. LiTong maintains strategic inventory of high-demand Rubycon series to support urgent requirements. For large volume orders, we recommend discussing demand forecasting and scheduling agreements with our sales team to ensure continuous supply. Contact our sales team for current lead times on specific part numbers.
faqs.decisionGuide: Contact our sales team with your forecasted volumes and delivery requirements.

faqs.question: Does Rubycon offer automotive-qualified capacitors?
faqs.answer: Yes, Rubycon offers a comprehensive portfolio of automotive-qualified aluminum electrolytic capacitors. Their automotive-grade capacitors are qualified to AEC-Q200 standards and are suitable for critical automotive applications including engine control units, safety systems, LED lighting, and ADAS. Rubycon's automotive capacitors feature extended temperature ranges up to 150C, enhanced vibration resistance, and long operational life required by automotive applications. All automotive-qualified capacitors undergo rigorous testing including temperature cycling, mechanical shock, and high-temperature operational life testing. Rubycon's IATF 16949 certification ensures consistent quality in automotive component manufacturing. The company provides comprehensive PPAP documentation support for automotive customers.
faqs.decisionGuide: Contact our automotive FAE specialist for component recommendations and qualification support.

faqs.question: How do I request samples for evaluation?
faqs.answer: LiTong provides sample support for Rubycon capacitor evaluation. To request samples, contact our sales team with the specific part numbers and quantities you need. Please include information about your application, projected annual usage, and target production timeline. This helps us prioritize sample requests and provide appropriate technical support. Standard sample quantities are typically 2-5 pieces per part number. For specialized or high-value capacitors, sample availability may be subject to review. We aim to ship standard samples within 3-5 business days. For urgent requirements, please indicate the critical timeline and we will do our best to accommodate.
faqs.decisionGuide: Contact our sales team with your sample requirements and application information.

faqs.question: What storage and handling requirements apply to Rubycon capacitors?
faqs.answer: Rubycon recommends storing aluminum electrolytic capacitors at 5-35C temperature and less than 75% relative humidity. The maximum recommended storage period is 3 years from the manufacturing date. Capacitors should be stored in their original packaging away from direct sunlight, corrosive gases, and dust. Avoid storing near heat sources or in high-humidity environments. Capacitors stored beyond 3 years may require reformation - applying rated voltage through a current-limiting resistor for several hours to rebuild the oxide layer. Always follow first-in-first-out inventory practices. For high-reliability applications, consider testing stored capacitors before use. Proper handling during assembly is also important to avoid mechanical damage to leads or case.
faqs.decisionGuide: Follow recommended storage conditions and use capacitors within 3 years of manufacture.

articles.title: Aluminum Electrolytic Capacitor Selection Guide
articles.summary: Comprehensive guide to selecting aluminum electrolytic capacitors for various applications. Covers capacitance, voltage rating, temperature, ripple current, and lifetime considerations.
content: Aluminum electrolytic capacitors are essential components in power electronics, providing energy storage, filtering, and decoupling functions. Proper selection is critical for reliable operation and long service life.

The required capacitance depends on the application. For power supply output filtering, calculate based on allowable ripple voltage and load current. For DC bus applications, consider both ripple requirements and energy storage needs.

Always apply voltage derating for reliability. A general rule is to operate at 70-80% of the rated voltage. This significantly extends capacitor lifetime - operating at 80% of rated voltage can double the expected life compared to 100% voltage.

Select capacitors with temperature ratings appropriate for your application environment. Standard ratings are 85C and 105C. Higher temperature ratings provide longer life at the same operating temperature due to the Arrhenius relationship.

Ripple current causes self-heating in capacitors. Calculate the expected ripple current in your application and select capacitors with adequate ripple current rating. Always include margin - at least 20% above calculated values.

Consider the expected service life of your product. Standard capacitors offer 2000-5000 hours at rated temperature, while long-life series provide 10000-12000 hours. Calculate expected lifetime at your actual operating conditions.
For output filtering, prioritize low ESR for ripple reduction. For DC bus applications, ensure adequate ripple current capability and consider parallel configurations for high-current designs.

DC bus capacitors must handle high ripple current from inverter switching. Select snap-in or screw terminal types for adequate current capability. Consider voltage transients from regenerative braking.
Use AEC-Q200 qualified capacitors for automotive applications. Ensure adequate temperature margin for under-hood environments. Consider vibration requirements.
- Selecting based only on capacitance value
Contact our FAE team for assistance with your specific application requirements.

faeInsights.insight: In my 15 years of supporting capacitor applications, I've seen many designs fail due to improper capacitor selection. The most common mistake is focusing only on capacitance value while ignoring ripple current requirements. I always tell engineers to calculate the actual operating conditions first - temperature, voltage, and ripple current - then select capacitors with appropriate margins. The extra time spent on proper selection pays dividends in field reliability.
faeInsights.logic: The capacitor selection process should follow a systematic approach: define application requirements, calculate operating conditions, identify candidate series, verify all specifications, and apply appropriate derating. This ensures reliable operation over the product lifetime.
keyTakeaways: Always calculate actual operating conditions before selecting capacitors
Apply voltage derating of at least 20% for reliability
Include 20% margin on ripple current ratings
Consider temperature effects on lifetime
Select series appropriate for your application type
commonPitfalls: Selecting based only on capacitance value
bestPractices: Perform thorough operating condition analysis
Use manufacturer lifetime models for prediction
Implement proper thermal management
Consider parallel configurations for high current
Validate selections with prototype testing

customerCases.challenge: The customer experienced capacitor failures in their power supply after 2 years of field operation. The failures were traced to inadequate ripple current capability and high operating temperatures.
customerCases.solution: We recommended upgrading to Rubycon USG series capacitors with 50% higher ripple current rating. Thermal design was improved with additional airflow and better PCB layout. Voltage derating was increased from 15% to 25%.
customerCases.results: The redesigned power supply achieved calculated lifetime of 15+ years. After 3 years in field operation, zero capacitor failures have been reported across 5000+ units.
customerCases.feedback: The FAE team's guidance on capacitor selection and thermal design was invaluable. We now apply these principles to all our power supply designs.

faqs.question: What is the most important parameter when selecting capacitors?
faqs.answer: While capacitance value is important, ripple current capability and temperature rating are often more critical for reliability. Ripple current causes self-heating, which dramatically affects lifetime. A capacitor with adequate capacitance but insufficient ripple current rating will fail prematurely. Similarly, operating temperature has an exponential effect on lifetime - every 10C reduction doubles life. Always verify that your selected capacitor can handle the actual ripple current and temperature in your application, with appropriate margins.
faqs.decisionGuide: Prioritize ripple current and temperature ratings alongside capacitance for reliable selection.

faqs.question: How much voltage derating should I apply?
faqs.answer: Industry best practice recommends operating aluminum electrolytic capacitors at 70-80% of their rated voltage. This means for a 24V application, use a 35V or 50V rated capacitor. The benefits of voltage derating are significant: operating at 80% of rated voltage typically doubles the expected lifetime compared to 100% voltage. For critical applications or high-reliability designs, consider 50% derating (2x the operating voltage). The small additional cost of higher voltage rated capacitors is usually justified by the improved reliability and extended lifetime.
faqs.decisionGuide: Apply minimum 20% voltage derating; consider 50% derating for critical applications.

faqs.question: What temperature rating should I choose?
faqs.answer: Select capacitors with temperature ratings higher than your maximum expected ambient temperature. For applications up to 70C ambient, 85C rated capacitors are usually sufficient. For applications above 70C or with significant self-heating, use 105C rated capacitors. The higher temperature rating provides longer life at the same operating temperature due to the Arrhenius relationship. For example, a 105C rated capacitor operating at 70C will have approximately 4x the lifetime of an 85C rated capacitor at the same temperature. The cost difference is typically small compared to the reliability benefit.
faqs.decisionGuide: Use 105C rated capacitors for high-temperature applications or where extended life is required.

faqs.question: How do I calculate required ripple current capability?
faqs.answer: To calculate required ripple current capability, first determine the actual ripple current in your application. For power supplies, this depends on load current, switching frequency, and duty cycle. For motor drives, it depends on motor current and PWM strategy. Once you have the calculated ripple current, select capacitors with a rating at least 20% higher to provide margin. Also consider temperature derating - the ripple current rating decreases at higher ambient temperatures. For example, at 85C ambient, the usable ripple current may be only 70-80% of the 105C rating.
faqs.decisionGuide: Calculate actual ripple current and select capacitors with 20% margin, accounting for temperature derating.

faqs.question: When should I use long-life series capacitors?
faqs.answer: Long-life series capacitors (such as Rubycon YXJ, USG, or LXM) are recommended when: 1) Extended service life is required (15+ years), 2) Maintenance access is difficult or costly, 3) High reliability is critical (medical, aerospace, safety systems), 4) Operating temperatures are high, or 5) The cost of failure exceeds the additional component cost. Long-life series typically offer 20-100% longer operational life than standard series. While the initial cost is 10-30% higher, the total cost of ownership is often lower due to reduced maintenance and higher reliability.
faqs.decisionGuide: Use long-life series for critical applications, difficult maintenance, or extended service life requirements.

articles.title: Ripple Current Calculations and Thermal Management
articles.summary: Detailed guide to calculating ripple current in capacitor applications and managing thermal performance. Includes formulas, examples, and practical design recommendations.
content: Ripple current is a critical parameter in aluminum electrolytic capacitor applications. Excessive ripple current causes self-heating, which reduces capacitor lifetime and can lead to premature failure. Proper calculation and thermal management are essential for reliable designs.

Ripple current is the AC current flowing through a capacitor due to charging and discharging cycles. In power supplies, this results from the switching action of converters. In motor drives, it comes from the inverter PWM switching. The RMS value of this AC current determines the heating in the capacitor.
For buck converters, the output ripple current is approximately:
I_ripple = V_out x (V_in - V_out) / (V_in x f_sw x L)
Where V_out is output voltage, V_in is input voltage, f_sw is switching frequency, and L is inductance.
For DC bus capacitors, ripple current depends on load characteristics and rectifier type. For three-phase rectifiers:
For single-phase rectifiers, ripple current is higher:
Motor drive DC bus ripple current depends on motor current, DC bus voltage, and PWM strategy. A conservative estimate is:
The temperature rise in a capacitor due to ripple current is:
dT = I_ripple^2 x ESR x R_th
Where I_ripple is RMS ripple current, ESR is equivalent series resistance, and R_th is thermal resistance.
For a capacitor with 2A ripple current, 0.1 ohm ESR, and 15C/W thermal resistance:
If ambient temperature is 50C, the capacitor core temperature is 56C.
Ensure adequate clearance around capacitors (minimum 10mm) for airflow. Orient capacitors vertically to promote chimney effect cooling.
For high-ripple applications, forced air cooling can reduce capacitor temperature by 10-20C, significantly extending lifetime.
Using multiple capacitors in parallel distributes ripple current and reduces individual capacitor heating. Two capacitors in parallel halve the ripple current in each.
Large screw terminal capacitors can be mounted on heat sinks for improved thermal performance. Ensure good thermal contact with thermal interface material.
- Keep capacitor case temperature below 85C for optimal lifetime
- Provide at least 20% margin on ripple current ratings
- Consider temperature derating in ripple current calculations
- Use parallel configurations for high-ripple applications
- Implement temperature monitoring for critical applications

faeInsights.insight: Ripple current is often the most misunderstood parameter in capacitor applications. Many engineers calculate the capacitance needed for ripple voltage but forget to verify ripple current capability. I've seen countless failures from this oversight. The key is to remember that ripple current causes self-heating, and temperature is what really determines capacitor life. Always calculate the temperature rise from ripple current and ensure it stays within acceptable limits.
faeInsights.logic: The relationship between ripple current, ESR, and temperature rise is fundamental to capacitor reliability. By understanding this relationship and implementing proper thermal management, designers can ensure long capacitor life even in demanding applications.
keyTakeaways: Always calculate ripple current and verify capacitor ratings
Temperature rise from ripple current is the key reliability factor
Use parallel capacitors to distribute thermal load
Implement proper thermal management for high-ripple applications
Monitor capacitor temperature during testing
commonPitfalls: Ignoring ripple current in capacitor selection
Not accounting for ESR variation with temperature
bestPractices: Calculate ripple current for worst-case conditions
Include 20% margin on ripple current ratings
Measure actual capacitor temperature during testing
Use thermal imaging to identify hot spots
Consider parallel configurations for high current

customerCases.challenge: The customer experienced capacitor failures in their motor drive after 18 months of operation. Analysis showed the capacitors were operating at 95C, well above their rating.
customerCases.solution: Ripple current calculations revealed the DC bus capacitors were handling 25A RMS, exceeding their 20A rating. Poor thermal design with inadequate airflow contributed to high operating temperatures. We recommended adding a third capacitor in parallel, reducing individual ripple current to 16.7A. Forced air cooling was implemented, reducing ambient temperature from 55C to 45C.
customerCases.results: Capacitor temperature dropped to 75C, well within rating. Calculated lifetime increased from 3 years to over 15 years. No failures have occurred in 4 years of subsequent operation.
customerCases.feedback: The detailed ripple current analysis and thermal recommendations completely transformed our design approach. We now include thermal analysis in all our drive designs.

faqs.question: What is ESR and why is it important?
faqs.answer: ESR (Equivalent Series Resistance) is the internal resistance of a capacitor. It's important because it determines how much heat is generated by ripple current. Power dissipation in the capacitor is P = I^2 x ESR. Higher ESR means more heating for the same ripple current. ESR varies with temperature and frequency - it's typically higher at low temperatures and low frequencies. When selecting capacitors for high-ripple applications, look for low ESR series such as Rubycon ZLH. Always use the ESR value at your operating frequency and temperature for accurate calculations.
faqs.decisionGuide: Select low-ESR series for high-ripple applications; use ESR values at operating conditions for calculations.

faqs.question: How does temperature affect ESR?
faqs.answer: ESR varies significantly with temperature. At -40C, ESR can be 3-5 times higher than at room temperature. At 105C, ESR is typically 20-30% lower than at 20C. This temperature dependence is important for two reasons: First, cold start conditions may result in higher ESR and more heating until the capacitor warms up. Second, as a capacitor heats up from ripple current, its ESR decreases, which can create a stabilizing effect. However, the overall temperature still increases lifetime degradation. For worst-case calculations, use the ESR at maximum expected temperature.
faqs.decisionGuide: Use worst-case ESR values for thermal calculations; consider cold-start conditions.

faqs.question: Can I measure ripple current in my application?
faqs.answer: Yes, ripple current can be measured using a current probe and oscilloscope. Place the current probe in series with the capacitor lead. Measure the AC current waveform and calculate the RMS value. For accurate measurements, use a true RMS meter or calculate RMS from the waveform. Be aware that ripple current often contains multiple frequency components from switching harmonics. For thermal calculations, you need the total RMS current including all harmonics. Some power analyzers can measure this directly. Always measure under worst-case operating conditions (maximum load, minimum input voltage, etc.).
faqs.decisionGuide: Measure ripple current under worst-case conditions using appropriate equipment.

faqs.question: What is the relationship between ripple current and lifetime?
faqs.answer: Ripple current affects lifetime through self-heating. Higher ripple current causes more heating (P = I^2 x ESR), which increases capacitor core temperature. Since capacitor lifetime follows the Arrhenius equation, every 10C increase in temperature approximately halves the lifetime. For example, if a capacitor is rated for 10000 hours at 105C, but ripple current raises its temperature to 115C, the expected lifetime would be approximately 5000 hours. This is why proper ripple current derating and thermal management are critical for long capacitor life.
faqs.decisionGuide: Minimize temperature rise from ripple current through proper selection and thermal management.

faqs.question: How do multiple frequencies affect ripple current heating?
faqs.answer: When ripple current contains multiple frequency components (common in switching power supplies), each component contributes to heating based on its RMS value and the ESR at that frequency. The total heating is the sum of heating from all frequency components: P_total = I_1^2 x ESR_1 + I_2^2 x ESR_2 + ... Since ESR typically decreases with increasing frequency, high-frequency components may contribute less heating than low-frequency components of the same amplitude. For accurate thermal calculations, analyze the ripple current spectrum and calculate heating for each significant frequency component.
faqs.decisionGuide: Analyze ripple current spectrum for accurate thermal calculations in high-frequency applications.

articles.title: Capacitor Lifetime Calculation and Prediction
articles.summary: Comprehensive guide to calculating and predicting aluminum electrolytic capacitor lifetime. Covers the Arrhenius equation, voltage derating effects, and practical calculation methods.
content: Predicting capacitor lifetime is essential for designing reliable electronic systems. Aluminum electrolytic capacitor lifetime depends primarily on operating temperature and voltage stress. Understanding these relationships enables engineers to design for required service life.
Capacitor lifetime follows the Arrhenius relationship, which describes how chemical reaction rates (including electrolyte evaporation) vary with temperature:
- Lx = Expected lifetime at temperature Tx
- Lr = Rated lifetime at reference temperature Tr
This equation shows that lifetime approximately doubles for every 10C decrease in temperature.
Operating voltage also affects lifetime. The relationship is:
- Lv = Lifetime at operating voltage Vx
- Lr = Lifetime at rated voltage Vr
Practically, operating at 80% of rated voltage approximately doubles lifetime compared to 100% voltage.
When both temperature and voltage derating are applied, the effects multiply:
Consider a 105C rated capacitor with 10000 hour rated lifetime:
Capacitor end-of-life is typically defined as:
These criteria represent the point where capacitor performance may affect circuit operation.

Manufacturers perform accelerated life tests at elevated temperatures to verify lifetime predictions. These tests follow standardized procedures (such as IEC 60384-4) and provide confidence in lifetime models.
Actual field lifetime may vary based on:
- Actual operating conditions vs. design assumptions
- Design for at least 2x the required service life to account for uncertainties
- Measure actual operating temperature during worst-case testing
- Implement voltage derating of at least 20%
- Consider parallel configurations to reduce individual capacitor stress
- Plan for capacitor replacement in long-life applications

faeInsights.insight: Lifetime calculation is both a science and an art. The Arrhenius equation provides a good foundation, but real-world factors can cause significant variations. I always recommend designing for at least double the required lifetime to account for uncertainties. Temperature measurement is critical - a 5C error in temperature estimate can result in 30-40% error in lifetime prediction. Always measure actual operating conditions during worst-case testing, and consider implementing condition monitoring for critical applications.
faeInsights.logic: The key to accurate lifetime prediction is understanding all the factors that affect capacitor degradation and applying appropriate safety margins. Temperature is the dominant factor, but voltage stress, ripple current, and environmental conditions all play important roles.
keyTakeaways: Temperature is the dominant factor in capacitor lifetime
Design for 2x required lifetime to account for uncertainties
Measure actual operating conditions during testing
Apply both temperature and voltage derating
Consider condition monitoring for critical applications
commonPitfalls: Not accounting for ripple current heating
bestPractices: Measure temperature at worst-case operating conditions
Use thermal imaging to identify hot spots
Apply 20% voltage derating minimum
Design for 2x required service life
Implement condition monitoring when possible

customerCases.challenge: The customer required 15-year service life for their medical device but was unsure if standard capacitors could meet this requirement.
customerCases.solution: Analysis of operating conditions showed 45C internal temperature with 24V operation. Original design used 35V rated capacitors with minimal derating. We recommended upgrading to 50V rated capacitors (52% derating) and implementing improved thermal design. This provided significant lifetime extension through voltage derating.
customerCases.results: Calculated lifetime increased from 12 years to over 25 years. The design passed accelerated life testing and has been in field operation for 5 years with zero failures.
customerCases.feedback: The detailed lifetime analysis gave us confidence in our design. The voltage derating approach was a revelation - we now apply this to all our medical device designs.

faqs.question: What is the typical end-of-life for aluminum electrolytic capacitors?
faqs.answer: Aluminum electrolytic capacitors typically reach end-of-life through gradual degradation rather than sudden failure. The standard end-of-life criteria are: 1) Capacitance reduction of 20% from initial value, 2) ESR increase of 2x from initial value, or 3) Leakage current exceeding specifications. These changes occur gradually over time due to electrolyte evaporation. A capacitor at end-of-life may still function but with degraded performance. In power supply applications, this may manifest as increased output ripple or reduced hold-up time. For critical applications, consider replacing capacitors before they reach end-of-life criteria.
faqs.decisionGuide: Monitor capacitor parameters over time; plan replacement before end-of-life for critical applications.

faqs.question: How accurate are lifetime calculations?
faqs.answer: Lifetime calculations based on the Arrhenius equation are generally accurate within +/- 30% when operating conditions are well understood. The largest source of error is typically temperature measurement - a 5C error results in approximately 35% error in lifetime prediction. Other factors affecting accuracy include: actual vs. rated ESR, voltage stress assumptions, and environmental factors. For critical applications, it's recommended to: 1) Measure actual operating temperature during worst-case testing, 2) Apply safety margins (design for 2x required life), 3) Consider accelerated life testing of prototypes, and 4) Implement condition monitoring in the final product.
faqs.decisionGuide: Apply safety margins and validate with testing; implement monitoring for critical applications.

faqs.question: Can capacitors be replaced to extend product life?
faqs.answer: Yes, capacitor replacement is a common and effective way to extend product life. Aluminum electrolytic capacitors are typically the limiting factor in power supply lifetime, so replacing them can effectively reset the product's expected life. When planning for replaceable capacitors: 1) Use through-hole or snap-in mounting for easier replacement, 2) Document capacitor specifications and replacement procedures, 3) Consider using higher-grade capacitors for replacement to extend service life further, and 4) Implement condition monitoring to determine optimal replacement timing. For industrial equipment, planned capacitor replacement every 10-15 years is often more cost-effective than designing for 20+ year capacitor life.
faqs.decisionGuide: Plan for capacitor replacement in long-life products; use accessible mounting styles.

faqs.question: What is the shelf life of unused capacitors?
faqs.answer: Rubycon recommends a maximum storage period of 3 years at room temperature (5-35C) for aluminum electrolytic capacitors. During storage, the electrolyte slowly evaporates and the oxide layer may degrade slightly. Capacitors stored beyond 3 years may require reformation - applying rated voltage through a current-limiting resistor for several hours to rebuild the oxide layer. High temperature or humidity during storage accelerates degradation. For critical applications, consider testing stored capacitors before use. Always check the manufacturing date code and follow first-in-first-out inventory practices.
faqs.decisionGuide: Use capacitors within 3 years of manufacture; perform reformation if stored longer.

faqs.question: How do I implement capacitor condition monitoring?
faqs.answer: Capacitor condition monitoring can be implemented through several methods: 1) ESR monitoring - measure capacitor ESR periodically using specialized equipment or built-in circuitry, 2) Ripple voltage monitoring - increased output ripple can indicate capacitor degradation, 3) Temperature monitoring - track capacitor temperature over time, 4) Leakage current monitoring - measure capacitor leakage current. For DC bus applications, monitoring the AC ripple voltage can provide indication of capacitor health. Some advanced motor drives implement DC bus ripple analysis to detect capacitor degradation. Implement warning thresholds at 50% of end-of-life criteria and replacement thresholds at 80%.
faqs.decisionGuide: Implement condition monitoring for critical applications to predict maintenance needs.

articles.title: Automotive Capacitor Selection and AEC-Q200 Qualification
articles.summary: Guide to selecting and qualifying capacitors for automotive applications. Covers AEC-Q200 requirements, temperature ratings, and reliability considerations for automotive electronics.
content: Automotive electronics present unique challenges for capacitor selection. Extreme temperature ranges, high reliability requirements, and long service life expectations demand careful component selection and qualification.
AEC-Q200 is the automotive qualification standard for passive components. It defines rigorous testing requirements including:
- High temperature storage: 1000 hours at maximum rated temperature
- Temperature humidity bias: 1000 hours at 85C/85% RH
- High temperature operational life: 1000 hours at rated temperature
Rubycon offers AEC-Q200 qualified capacitors specifically designed for automotive applications:
Requirements: 150C capability, high reliability, long life
Recommended: BXA or BXB series depending on location
Requirements: High reliability, functional safety support
Recommended: BXA series with full AEC-Q200 qualification
Requirements: High voltage, high ripple current, extreme reliability
Recommended: Specialized high-voltage series with automotive qualification
- Consider load dump transients (up to 174V for 12V systems)
- Use capacitors with enhanced mechanical construction
LiTong provides full documentation support for automotive customers.

faeInsights.insight: Automotive capacitor selection goes beyond just meeting AEC-Q200 requirements. The extreme temperature cycles in automotive applications - from -40C cold start to 150C under-hood - create significant stress on capacitors. I always recommend extensive temperature cycling testing beyond the AEC-Q200 minimums. Also, don't forget about vibration - automotive environments have severe vibration that can cause mechanical failures if not properly addressed. The cost of a field failure in automotive is extremely high, so invest in the highest reliability components and thorough testing.
faeInsights.logic: Automotive applications require a holistic approach to capacitor selection, considering electrical, thermal, mechanical, and reliability requirements. AEC-Q200 qualification is the baseline, but successful automotive designs go beyond these minimums.
keyTakeaways: Use AEC-Q200 qualified capacitors for all automotive applications
Apply higher voltage derating (30-40%) for automotive
Consider extreme temperature cycling in design
Address vibration requirements with proper mounting
Plan for comprehensive documentation and traceability
commonPitfalls: Using commercial-grade capacitors in automotive applications
Insufficient voltage derating for load dump conditions
Inadequate temperature margin for under-hood applications
bestPractices: Use Grade 0 (150C) capacitors for under-hood applications
Implement 40% voltage derating for safety-critical applications
Perform extended temperature cycle testing
Use conformal coating for high-humidity environments
Maintain full traceability for automotive production

customerCases.challenge: The customer experienced capacitor failures in LED headlight modules during winter testing. Failures occurred during rapid temperature transitions from cold soak to operation.
customerCases.solution: Analysis revealed the standard capacitors were cracking due to thermal shock and CTE mismatch. The temperature transition from -40C to operating temperature occurred too rapidly for the capacitor construction. We recommended Rubycon BXA series capacitors with enhanced thermal shock resistance. Additional design changes included improved thermal management and gradual power-up sequencing.
customerCases.results: The redesigned module passed all thermal shock and temperature cycling tests. The design achieved AEC-Q200 Grade 0 qualification and has been in production for 3 years with zero field failures across 2 million vehicles.
customerCases.feedback: The automotive FAE support was critical to our success. The BXA series capacitors and design recommendations solved our thermal shock issues completely.

faqs.question: What is the difference between AEC-Q200 Grade 0 and Grade 1?
faqs.answer: AEC-Q200 Grade 0 and Grade 1 differ primarily in temperature range requirements. Grade 0 requires qualification from -40C to +150C, suitable for under-hood and extreme environment applications. Grade 1 requires -40C to +125C, suitable for most cabin and body electronics. Grade 0 testing is more stringent, including longer high-temperature exposure and more temperature cycles. Grade 0 capacitors typically cost 20-30% more than Grade 1 due to enhanced construction and additional testing. For under-hood applications, Grade 0 is mandatory. For cabin applications, Grade 1 is usually sufficient unless extreme temperature exposure is expected.
faqs.decisionGuide: Use Grade 0 for under-hood; Grade 1 is sufficient for most cabin applications.

faqs.question: What is PPAP and why is it required?
faqs.answer: PPAP (Production Part Approval Process) is a standardized process in the automotive industry to ensure suppliers can meet customer requirements. It includes documentation of design records, process flow, control plans, measurement system analysis, dimensional results, material testing, performance testing, and initial process studies. For capacitors, PPAP demonstrates that the component has been properly qualified, the manufacturing process is capable, and the part meets all specifications. Automotive OEMs typically require PPAP Level 3, which includes full documentation and samples. LiTong provides PPAP support for all automotive-qualified Rubycon capacitors.
faqs.decisionGuide: Request PPAP documentation early in the automotive design process.

faqs.question: How do I handle load dump conditions in automotive applications?
faqs.answer: Load dump is a severe voltage transient in automotive electrical systems that can reach 174V in 12V systems (or 348V in 24V systems) and last for hundreds of milliseconds. To handle load dump: 1) Select capacitors with voltage ratings significantly higher than nominal system voltage (minimum 50V for 12V systems), 2) Implement transient voltage suppression (TVS) devices, 3) Use capacitors with surge voltage ratings, 4) Consider the BXA series which is designed for automotive transients. For critical applications, implement active load dump protection circuits. Always test your design with actual load dump pulses per ISO 7637-2.
faqs.decisionGuide: Use 50V+ capacitors for 12V systems; implement TVS protection for load dump.

faqs.question: What vibration requirements apply to automotive capacitors?
faqs.answer: Automotive applications require capacitors to withstand significant vibration. AEC-Q200 specifies random vibration testing from 5-2000 Hz with acceleration up to 50G depending on the grade. For comparison, industrial applications typically see less than 5G. Automotive capacitors like the BXA series have enhanced mechanical construction including stronger lead attachments and improved internal connections. When designing for automotive vibration: 1) Use AEC-Q200 qualified capacitors, 2) Ensure proper mechanical mounting, 3) Avoid large radial lead capacitors in high-vibration areas, 4) Consider potting or conformal coating for additional support, 5) Test prototypes on vibration shakers.
faqs.decisionGuide: Use AEC-Q200 qualified capacitors with enhanced mechanical construction for automotive vibration.

faqs.question: Can I use commercial-grade capacitors in non-safety automotive applications?
faqs.answer: While it may be technically possible to use commercial-grade capacitors in some non-safety automotive applications, it is strongly discouraged. Automotive environments present extreme conditions - temperature cycling, vibration, humidity, and voltage transients - that commercial-grade capacitors are not designed to withstand. The cost savings from using commercial-grade parts are typically far outweighed by the risk of field failures, warranty costs, and potential liability. Automotive-grade capacitors undergo rigorous qualification testing that ensures reliability in these demanding conditions. For all automotive applications, use AEC-Q200 qualified capacitors from reputable manufacturers.
faqs.decisionGuide: Always use AEC-Q200 qualified capacitors for all automotive applications regardless of safety classification.
